text: Seneca draws a sharp distinction between living long and living much. A person can accumulate eighty years while inhabiting, in any meaningful sense, almost none of them — moving from appetite to appetite, never pausing to ask what any of it was for. He calls this existere sine vita, existing without truly living. By contrast, a life of even modest length that is fully inhabited — each hour weighed, each relationship chosen, each pleasure consciously received — constitutes what he considers a long life. Duration is a biological accident. Depth is an achievement. The Letters are, among other things, an argument that Lucilius still has time to choose depth.
question: What does Seneca mean by 'existere sine vita'?

options.text: Dying before one's natural lifespan has concluded
Living in exile far from one's homeland and community
Accumulating years without truly inhabiting any of them
Practicing philosophy without applying it to daily conduct
